content.problemStatement:
Dazzler has a task for you.

Given two **positive** integers AAA and BBB, find two **positive** integers iii and jjj such that:


gcd(i,j)>1(i,j) \gt 1(i,j)>1;
A≤i<j≤BA \leq i \lt j \leq BA≤i<j≤B;
The value (i+j)(i + j)(i+j) is **minimum** possible.
If there are multiple solutions, you may print any of them. If no such pair exists, print −1-1−1.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains of a single line of input, two integers AAA and BBB.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line two space-separated integers iii and jjj satisfying all the conditions. If no such pair exists, print −1-1−1.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated positive integers A and B (A < B).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output either two integers i and j (A ≤ i < j ≤ B, gcd(i, j) > 1) whose sum i + j is minimum, or -1 if no such pair exists. Print the answer for each test case on its own line.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There exists no pair satisfying all the conditions.
Test case 222: A valid pair satisfying all the conditions is (i,j)=(2,4)(i, j) = (2, 4)(i,j)=(2,4). The value gcd(2,4)=2>1\texttt{gcd}(2, 4) = 2 >1gcd(2,4)=2>1. The value (i+j)=6(i+j) = 6(i+j)=6.
It can be proven that no such pair exists that satisfies all the conditions and has sum less than 666.

solution.approach:
For a fixed i the smallest j > i that shares a divisor with i is i + d where d is the smallest divisor of i greater than 1 (the smallest prime factor, spf(i)). Therefore the optimal pair is the first i in the interval for which i + spf(i) ≤ B.  

*If A is even* then i = A is already the smallest possible i. The smallest divisor is 2, so the candidate pair is (A, A+2). If A+2 ≤ B we output it – it is optimal because any other i ≥ A would give a larger sum.  

*If A is odd* we compute spf(A). If A + spf(A) ≤ B we output (A, A+spf(A)). This pair uses the smallest possible i (A) and therefore has the minimum sum.  

If the above two attempts fail, the interval length is at most 2, so we can simply try all possible pairs (at most three numbers) and pick the one with gcd > 1 and minimal sum. If none exists we output -1.  

The only non‑constant work is finding spf(A) for an odd A. We pre‑compute all primes up to √10^9 ≈ 31623 using a simple sieve (≈3400 primes) and trial‑divide A by these primes; the first divisor found is spf(A). This is O(√A) in the worst case but very fast in practice and runs within limits for 10^5 test cases.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * P) where P is the number of primes up to 31623 (≈3400). In the common case the loop stops after a few divisions, so the practical runtime is well below 1 second for the maximal input.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(P) for storing the list of primes (≈3400 integers).
